key_insight: An optimization-based phase-field calibration can embed target nonequilibrium interface response functions, enabling accurate modeling of solute trapping, solute drag, and rapid-solidification morphology.
engineering_relevance: This gives alloy designers a practical way to predict microsegregation and growth patterns under rapid solidification using phase-field simulations with tunable interface kinetics.
method: Treat interfacial diffusivity interpolation as a free parameter for response matching.
Fit one-dimensional steady-state phase-field solutions to sharp-interface continuous-growth-model targets.
Extend the calibrated formulation to multicomponent dilute alloys with independently specified partition and liquidus responses.
evidence_signal: The calibrated model was validated against prescribed velocity-dependent response functions and then tested in 2D directional solidification, where increasing solute drag produced a progression from dendritic/cellular growth to banded morphologies.
watch_item: The approach relies on careful optimization of interfacial diffusivity in a large-interface phase-field model, so accuracy may depend on how well the target sharp-interface kinetics are known and enforced.
paper_title: Response-function-optimized phase field modeling of solute trapping and solute drag in rapid alloy solidification
